chapters.summary: Classification systems, five kingdoms, plant and animal kingdom — 3-4 questions annually in NEET.

topics.topicName: What is Living? Biodiversity and Need for Classification
topics.explanation: Characteristics of living organisms; biodiversity; need for classification; taxonomy and systematics; concept of species; taxonomical hierarchy (Species → Genus → Family → Order → Class → Phylum → Kingdom); binomial nomenclature (ICBN, ICZN).

Whittaker's five kingdom system; criteria: cell structure, body organisation, mode of nutrition, reproduction, phylogenetic relationships; salient features and classification of Monera, Protista and Fungi into major groups.
Lichens as symbiotic association; viruses — structure (TMV, bacteriophage), nucleic acid type, host range; viroids — RNA without protein coat; their significance.

topics.topicName: Plant Kingdom — Algae, Bryophytes, Pteridophytes, Gymnosperms
topics.explanation: Salient features and classification of each group (3-5 features + at least 2 examples each): Algae (Chlorophyceae, Phaeophyceae, Rhodophyceae); Bryophytes (liverworts, mosses); Pteridophytes (ferns); Gymnosperms (Cycas, Pinus).

Basis of classification; salient features of non-chordate phyla up to phyla level (Porifera, Coelenterata, Platyhelminthes, Nematoda, Annelida, Arthropoda, Mollusca, Echinodermata, Hemichordata) — 3-5 features + 2 examples each; chordates up to classes level (Cyclostomata, Chondrichthyes, Osteichthyes, Amphibia, Reptilia, Aves, Mammalia) — 3-5 features + 2 examples each.

chapters.chapterName: Structural Organisation in Animals and Plants
chapters.summary: Plant morphology, anatomy, tissues, plant families, animal tissues, frog anatomy — 3-4 questions annually in NEET.

topics.explanation: Morphology and modifications of: root (tap, fibrous, adventitious; modifications for storage, support, respiration); stem (modifications: rhizome, tuber, corm, bulb, tendril, thorn); leaf (modifications: tendrils, spines, phyllode, pitcher); inflorescence (racemose and cymose types); flower structure (calyx, corolla, androecium, gynoecium; placentation); fruit types; seed structure (dicot and monocot).

Distinguishing features, floral formula and floral diagram of: Malvaceae (cotton family), Cruciferae/Brassicaceae (mustard family), Leguminosae/Fabaceae (pea family), Compositae/Asteraceae (sunflower family), Graminae/Poaceae (grass family). Examples and economic importance.

Meristematic tissues: apical (root tip, shoot tip), lateral (vascular cambium, cork cambium), intercalary; simple permanent tissues: parenchyma, collenchyma, sclerenchyma; complex permanent tissues: xylem (tracheids, vessels, xylem fibres, xylem parenchyma) and phloem (sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem fibres, phloem parenchyma); tissue systems: epidermal, ground, vascular.

Internal structure of: dicot root (epidermis, cortex, endodermis with Casparian strips, pericycle, vascular bundles — radial arrangement, pith); monocot root; dicot stem (epidermis, hypodermis, cortex, pericycle, vascular bundles — open, conjoint, collateral — pith); monocot stem (scattered vascular bundles); dicot leaf (mesophyll: palisade and spongy parenchyma, vascular bundles, stomata); monocot leaf; secondary growth in dicot stem and root (cambium activity, annual rings, heartwood and sapwood).

Epithelial tissue: types (squamous, cuboidal, columnar, ciliated, glandular); connective tissue: types (loose — areolar, adipose; dense — tendon, ligament; specialised — cartilage, bone, blood); muscular tissue: skeletal, smooth, cardiac — structure and function; neural tissue: neuron structure, neuroglia.

topics.topicName: Morphology, Anatomy and Physiology of Frog
topics.explanation: External features of frog; internal anatomy: digestive system (mouth to cloaca, associated glands), circulatory system (three-chambered heart, portal systems), respiratory system (cutaneous, buccal, pulmonary), nervous system (brain regions, spinal cord, nerves), reproductive system (testes/ovaries, ducts) — brief account only.

chapters.summary: Cell organelles, biomolecules, enzymes, cell division — 4-5 questions annually in NEET.

topics.topicName: Cell Theory and Types of Cells
topics.explanation: Cell theory (Schleiden, Schwann, Virchow); cell as basic unit of life; prokaryotic cell (bacteria — cell wall, cell membrane, nucleoid, plasmid, ribosomes, flagella); eukaryotic cell — plant vs animal cell differences.

Cell envelope in bacteria (glycocalyx, cell wall, plasma membrane); plasma membrane — fluid mosaic model (Singer and Nicolson); active and passive transport; plant cell wall composition and structure.

Endoplasmic reticulum (SER and RER — structure and functions); Golgi apparatus (cisternae, secretion, glycosylation); lysosomes (origin, hydrolytic enzymes, autolysis); vacuoles (central vacuole in plants, contractile vacuole in protists, food vacuoles).

Mitochondria: double membrane, cristae, matrix, ATP synthesis; plastids: chloroplasts (grana, stroma, thylakoids), chromoplasts, leucoplasts; ribosomes: 70S (prokaryote) vs 80S (eukaryote); microbodies (peroxisomes, glyoxysomes); cytoskeleton: microtubules, microfilaments, intermediate filaments; cilia and flagella: 9+2 arrangement; centrioles: 9+0 triplet arrangement.

Nuclear envelope (double membrane, nuclear pores); chromatin organisation (euchromatin vs heterochromatin); chromosomes (structure, karyotype, autosomes vs sex chromosomes); nucleolus (rRNA synthesis).

Proteins: amino acid structure, peptide bond, levels of structure (primary to quaternary), denaturation; carbohydrates: monosaccharides, disaccharides, polysaccharides — structure and functions (starch, cellulose, glycogen); lipids: fats, oils, phospholipids, sterols — structure and biological role; nucleic acids: DNA double helix (Watson-Crick model), RNA types (mRNA, tRNA, rRNA).

Properties of enzymes (specificity, pH and temperature optima); enzyme action: lock-and-key model, induced-fit model; active site; cofactors, coenzymes, prosthetic groups; enzyme inhibition (competitive and non-competitive); classification and nomenclature (oxidoreductases, transferases, hydrolases, lyases, isomerases, ligases).

Cell cycle phases: G1, S, G2 (interphase) and M phase; mitosis: prophase (chromosome condensation), metaphase (plate formation), anaphase (chromatid separation), telophase (nuclear reconstitution), cytokinesis — significance; meiosis I (reduction division: synapsis, crossing over, chiasmata, bivalent) and meiosis II — significance (genetic variation, maintaining chromosome number).

chapters.summary: Photosynthesis (C3, C4, ETS), respiration (glycolysis, Krebs), plant hormones — 4-5 questions annually in NEET.

topics.explanation: Photosynthesis as autotrophic nutrition; site: chloroplasts (grana for light reactions, stroma for dark reactions); photosynthetic pigments: chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, carotenoids, xanthophylls (absorption spectra, action spectra); photochemical phase: photosystems I and II; Z-scheme of electron transport; non-cyclic photophosphorylation (produces ATP + NADPH + O₂); cyclic photophosphorylation (produces only ATP); photolysis of water.

topics.topicName: Photosynthesis — Dark Reactions and C3/C4 Pathways
topics.explanation: Calvin cycle (C3 pathway): CO₂ fixation by RuBisCO, 3-PGA, G3P, regeneration of RuBP; C4 pathway (Hatch-Slack cycle): PEP carboxylase, oxaloacetate, Kranz anatomy, bundle sheath cells; photorespiration (C3 plants — RuBisCO fixes O₂, energy wastage; absent in C4); factors affecting photosynthesis (light intensity, CO₂ concentration, temperature, water, light quality).

Mitchell's chemiosmotic hypothesis; proton gradient across thylakoid membrane; ATP synthase (CF₀-CF₁ complex); proton-motive force driving ATP synthesis — applicable to both chloroplasts and mitochondria.

Glycolysis (EMP pathway): location in cytoplasm; 10 steps overview; net gain: 2 ATP + 2 NADH + 2 pyruvate per glucose; fermentation (anaerobic): alcoholic fermentation (yeast — pyruvate → ethanol + CO₂) and lactic acid fermentation (muscle cells); energy yield comparison.

Link reaction: pyruvate → acetyl CoA (produces 1 NADH + CO₂ per pyruvate); Krebs/TCA cycle: location in mitochondrial matrix; per acetyl CoA: 3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP + 2 CO₂; electron transport system: complexes I-IV in inner mitochondrial membrane; oxidative phosphorylation; chemiosmosis; total ATP per glucose: ~36-38 ATP; respiratory quotient (RQ) for carbohydrates, fats, proteins.

Respiratory pathway as amphibolic (both anabolic and catabolic); respiratory intermediates as precursors for biosynthesis: acetyl CoA → fatty acids; alpha-ketoglutarate → amino acids; PEP → phenylalanine; G3P → glycerol.

Seed germination conditions; phases of growth: meristematic, elongation, maturation; arithmetic and geometric growth rates; S-shaped (sigmoid) and exponential growth curves; differentiation, dedifferentiation (callus), redifferentiation; totipotency.

Auxins (IAA): apical dominance, phototropism, geotropism, root initiation, fruit development; gibberellins (GA): seed germination, stem elongation, bolting, fruit development, delay of senescence; cytokinins (kinetin, zeatin): cell division, delay of senescence, apical dominance (antagonises auxin); ethylene: fruit ripening, abscission, triple response; ABA (abscisic acid): dormancy, stomatal closure, stress response — the 'stress hormone'.

chapters.summary: Digestion, breathing, circulation, excretion, locomotion, neural and chemical coordination — 8-10 questions annually in NEET. This is the highest-yield unit.

topics.explanation: Human respiratory system: nasal cavity, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, alveoli; mechanism of breathing: inspiration (diaphragm and intercostals contract) and expiration; regulation by respiratory centres (medulla oblongata — DRG, VRG), pneumotaxic centre, Hering-Breuer reflex; exchange of gases: partial pressure gradients (pO₂, pCO₂); transport of O₂ (oxyhaemoglobin, Bohr effect) and CO₂ (bicarbonate, carbaminohaemoglobin, dissolved); respiratory volumes: TV (500 mL), IRV, ERV, RV, IC, EC, FRC, VC, TLC; disorders: Asthma, Emphysema, Occupational respiratory disorders.

Blood composition: plasma (proteins — fibrinogen, albumin, globulin), RBCs (no nucleus, haemoglobin, 120-day life), WBCs (granulocytes — neutrophil, eosinophil, basophil; agranulocytes — monocyte, lymphocyte), platelets; blood groups: ABO (I^A, I^B, i alleles, codominance) and Rh (Rh factor); coagulation cascade; lymph composition and function; heart structure: four chambers, valves (tricuspid, bicuspid/mitral, pulmonary, aortic semilunar), conducting system (SA node — pacemaker, AV node, bundle of His, Purkinje fibres); cardiac cycle (0.8 sec): systole and diastole; cardiac output (SV × HR); ECG (P wave, QRS complex, T wave); double circulation (systemic + pulmonary); cardiac output regulation (Frank-Starling law, autonomic nervous system); disorders: Hypertension, Coronary artery disease, Angina pectoris, Heart failure.

Modes of excretion: ammonotelism (aquatic animals), ureotelism (mammals, amphibians), uricotelism (birds, reptiles, insects); human excretory system: kidneys (cortex, medulla, pelvis), ureters, urinary bladder, urethra; nephron structure: Bowman's capsule (glomerulus), PCT, loop of Henle (descending and ascending limbs), DCT, collecting duct; urine formation: ultrafiltration (GFR ~125 mL/min), selective reabsorption (Na⁺, glucose, amino acids, water), tubular secretion (H⁺, K⁺, NH₃); osmoregulation: counter-current mechanism (Henle's loop and vasa recta); regulation: Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, ANF (atrial natriuretic factor), ADH (vasopressin — water reabsorption in DCT and CD); Diabetes insipidus (no ADH); other excretory organs (skin, lungs, liver); disorders: Uraemia, Renal failure, Renal calculi, Nephritis; dialysis (haemodialysis).

Types of movement: amoeboid (pseudopodia), ciliary (respiratory tract, fallopian tube), flagellar (sperm), muscular; skeletal muscle structure: muscle fibre → myofibril → sarcomere (A band, I band, H zone, Z line, M line); contractile proteins: actin (thin filament — F-actin + tropomyosin + troponin) and myosin (thick filament — meromyosin heads); sliding filament theory of muscle contraction: cross-bridge cycle, role of Ca²⁺ and ATP; types of muscle contraction: isometric vs isotonic; skeletal system: axial (skull, vertebral column, ribs, sternum) and appendicular (pectoral girdle, upper limbs, pelvic girdle, lower limbs); joints: fibrous (immovable — sutures), cartilaginous (slightly movable), synovial (freely movable — ball-and-socket, hinge, pivot, gliding); disorders: Myasthenia gravis (autoimmune — acetylcholine receptors), Tetany (low Ca²⁺), Muscular dystrophy (genetic — dystrophin), Arthritis (inflammation of joints), Osteoporosis (low bone density), Gout (uric acid crystals in joints).

Neuron structure: dendrites, cell body (Nissl granules), axon, myelin sheath (Schwann cells), nodes of Ranvier; types: sensory, motor, interneuron; central nervous system: brain (cerebrum — grey matter/white matter/corpus callosum, cerebellum, medulla oblongata, pons, midbrain, thalamus, hypothalamus), spinal cord; peripheral nervous system: cranial nerves (12 pairs), spinal nerves (31 pairs); visceral/autonomic NS: sympathetic (fight-or-flight) and parasympathetic (rest-and-digest); generation of nerve impulse: resting membrane potential (-70 mV, Na⁺/K⁺ pump), action potential (depolarisation → repolarisation → hyperpolarisation); saltatory conduction in myelinated fibres; synapse: chemical synapse (neurotransmitters: acetylcholine, noradrenaline, dopamine, serotonin, GABA) — pre-synaptic → post-synaptic transmission; reflex arc.

Endocrine glands and their hormones; hypothalamus: releasing and inhibiting hormones (TRH, CRH, GnRH, GHRH, somatostatin, PRH, PIH); pituitary gland: anterior lobe — GH (dwarfism/gigantism/acromegaly), TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, Prolactin; posterior lobe — ADH (Diabetes insipidus), Oxytocin; pineal gland — melatonin; thyroid gland: T₃, T₄ (goiter, cretinism, exophthalmic goiter/Graves' disease), calcitonin; parathyroid: PTH (hypocalcaemia); adrenal cortex — mineralocorticoids (aldosterone), glucocorticoids (cortisol), sex corticoids; adrenal medulla — adrenaline, noradrenaline; pancreas: insulin (β cells — diabetes mellitus), glucagon (α cells); gonads: testosterone (testes), oestrogen and progesterone (ovary); mechanism of hormone action: lipid-soluble (nuclear receptors) and water-soluble (second messenger — cAMP pathway).

chapters.summary: Plant reproduction, human reproduction, reproductive health — 4-5 questions annually in NEET.

topics.topicName: Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants — Flower Structure and Gametophytes
topics.explanation: Parts of a flower; stamen structure (anther — microsporangia, microspore mother cell, microsporogenesis, pollen grain wall: exine and intine, pollen viability); pistil structure (stigma, style, ovary, ovule — funiculus, hilum, integuments, nucellus, micropyle, chalaza); megasporogenesis; female gametophyte: Polygonum type (8-nucleate, 7-celled embryo sac — egg cell, 2 synergids, 3 antipodals, 2 polar nuclei).

topics.topicName: Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants — Pollination and Fertilisation
topics.explanation: Pollination types: autogamy, geitonogamy, xenogamy; agencies: anemophily, entomophily, hydrophily, zoophily — adaptations and examples; outbreeding devices (dichogamy, herkogamy, self-incompatibility); pollen-pistil interaction (pollen recognition, compatible vs incompatible); double fertilisation: siphonogamy, syngamy (sperm + egg → zygote), triple fusion (sperm + 2 polar nuclei → triploid PEN → endosperm); post-fertilisation: development of endosperm (nuclear, cellular, helobial types), embryo (proembryo → globular → heart → torpedo), seed development, fruit formation; special modes: apomixis, parthenocarpy, polyembryony; significance of seed and fruit.

topics.topicName: Human Reproduction — Male and Female Reproductive Systems
topics.explanation: Male system: testes (seminiferous tubules, Sertoli cells, Leydig cells), epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles, prostate, bulbourethral glands, penis; microscopic anatomy of testis; female system: ovaries (ovarian follicles), fallopian tubes (infundibulum, fimbria, ampulla, isthmus), uterus (perimetrium, myometrium, endometrium), cervix, vagina; microscopic anatomy of ovary.

topics.topicName: Human Reproduction — Gametogenesis and Menstrual Cycle
topics.explanation: Spermatogenesis: spermatogonia → primary spermatocyte → secondary spermatocytes → spermatids → sperms (spermiation, spermiation); spermatozoon structure (head, neck, middle piece, tail); oogenesis: oogonia → primary oocyte (arrested in prophase I) → released at ovulation as secondary oocyte → fertilised → completes meiosis II; menstrual cycle (28 days): menstrual phase (days 1-5), follicular/proliferative phase (days 1-13, FSH → follicle growth → oestrogen), ovulatory phase (day 14, LH surge → ovulation), luteal/secretory phase (days 15-28, LH → corpus luteum → progesterone).

topics.topicName: Human Reproduction — Fertilisation and Embryonic Development
topics.explanation: Fertilisation in ampulla of fallopian tube; acrosome reaction; cortical reaction (block to polyspermy); zygote formation; embryonic development: cleavage (mitotic divisions, no growth) → morula (16-32 cells) → blastocyst (inner cell mass + trophoblast + blastocoel); implantation (blastocyst embeds in endometrium, day 7); pregnancy: hCG (maintains corpus luteum), placenta formation and functions (nutrition, gas exchange, waste removal, hormone secretion); parturition (role of oxytocin, relaxin); lactation (prolactin → milk production; oxytocin → milk ejection); colostrum (IgA antibodies).

Need for reproductive health; STD prevention (HIV/AIDS, gonorrhoea, syphilis, hepatitis B, chlamydia); birth control methods: natural (periodic abstinence, withdrawal, lactational amenorrhoea), barrier (condoms, diaphragm, cervical cap, vaults), IUDs (copper-T, hormone-releasing), pills (combined oral, progesterone-only), injectable, implants, surgical (vasectomy, tubectomy); MTP (Medical Termination of Pregnancy) — legal and medical aspects; amniocentesis (sex determination, chromosomal disorders — legal restrictions); infertility: causes; ART: IVF-ET (in vitro fertilisation — embryo transfer), ZIFT (zygote intrafallopian transfer), GIFT (gamete intrafallopian transfer), ICSI, surrogacy.

chapters.summary: Mendel's laws, molecular genetics, evolution — 5-6 questions annually in NEET.

topics.explanation: Mendel's experiments on Pisum sativum; Law of Dominance; Law of Segregation (monohybrid cross, 3:1 ratio); Law of Independent Assortment (dihybrid cross, 9:3:3:1 ratio); test cross; back cross; phenotypic and genotypic ratios.

Incomplete dominance (4 o'clock plant — 1:2:1 phenotypic ratio); co-dominance (ABO blood groups — I^A, I^B codominant, i recessive; transfusion compatibility); multiple alleles (ABO blood group system); pleiotropy (one gene → multiple traits; sickle cell anaemia); polygenic inheritance (skin colour, height — multiple gene pairs, continuous variation).

Chromosome theory of inheritance (Sutton and Boveri); chromosomes as carriers of genes; sex determination: XX-XY in humans (males = XY, sex-determining region on Y — SRY gene); ZW-ZZ in birds (females = ZW); XO in grasshopper; haplodiploidy in honey bees; sex-linked inheritance: haemophilia (X-linked recessive — factor VIII deficiency, royal disease), colour blindness (X-linked recessive); linkage (Morgan — Drosophila experiments); crossing over; recombination frequency as measure of map distance.

Mendelian disorders: thalassemia (autosomal recessive — defective haemoglobin synthesis, alpha and beta thalassemia); chromosomal disorders: Down's syndrome (trisomy 21 — 47 chromosomes, mental retardation, characteristic features, maternal age effect); Turner's syndrome (45, XO — sterile female, rudimentary ovaries, short stature); Klinefelter's syndrome (47, XXY — sterile male, gynaecomastia); Cri-du-chat (deletion of chromosome 5p).

topics.topicName: Molecular Basis of Inheritance — DNA Structure and Replication
topics.explanation: Search for genetic material: Griffith's transformation experiment, Avery-MacLeod-McCarty experiment, Hershey-Chase experiment (DNA is genetic material); DNA structure: Watson-Crick double helix model (antiparallel strands, base pairing A-T and G-C, major and minor grooves); Chargaff's rules; DNA packaging: nucleosome (histone octamer + 146 bp DNA), solenoid, chromosome; DNA replication: semi-conservative (Meselson-Stahl experiment); enzymes: DNA polymerase (5'→3'), helicase, primase, ligase, topoisomerase; leading and lagging strand synthesis; Okazaki fragments.

topics.topicName: Molecular Basis of Inheritance — Transcription, Translation and Gene Regulation
topics.explanation: Central dogma (DNA → RNA → Protein); transcription: template strand, RNA polymerase, promoter, transcription unit, 5'→3' synthesis; hnRNA processing in eukaryotes (capping, tailing, splicing — introns and exons); genetic code: codon (triplet), 64 codons (61 sense + 3 stop), degeneracy, universality, non-overlapping, non-ambiguous; start codon AUG (methionine); stop codons UAA, UAG, UGA; tRNA structure (cloverleaf, anticodon, CCA-3' end); translation: initiation, elongation (peptide bond by peptidyl transferase), termination; gene regulation: Lac Operon in E. coli (Jacob-Monod model) — structural genes (lacZ, lacY, lacA), operator, promoter, regulator gene (lac I — repressor protein), inducer (allolactose), inducible operon.

topics.topicName: Human Genome Project and DNA Fingerprinting
topics.explanation: Human Genome Project: goals (sequence all ~3×10⁹ bp), findings (25,000 genes, 1.1 million SNPs, repetitive DNA), significance; DNA fingerprinting (Alec Jeffreys): principle (Variable Number of Tandem Repeats — VNTRs, Southern blotting, DNA probes, autoradiography); applications: forensics, paternity testing, population studies; protein biosynthesis overview.

topics.topicName: Origin of Life and Biological Evolution
topics.explanation: Origin of life: Big Bang, chemical evolution (Oparin-Haldane hypothesis — primordial soup), Miller-Urey experiment (amino acids from inorganic molecules); first cells (coacervates, microspheres); evidences for evolution: palaeontology (fossils, geological time scale), comparative anatomy (homologous organs — divergent evolution; analogous organs — convergent evolution; vestigial organs), comparative embryology (von Baer's law), molecular evidence (DNA/protein homology, cytochrome c).

Lamarckism (inheritance of acquired characters — incorrect); Darwinism: natural selection, variation, survival of the fittest, examples (Industrial melanism in Biston betularia, antibiotic resistance); Modern Synthetic Theory (Neo-Darwinism): mutation + natural selection + genetic drift + gene flow + reproductive isolation; types of natural selection: directional, stabilising (normalising), disruptive; Hardy-Weinberg principle: allele frequency equilibrium (p + q = 1; p² + 2pq + q² = 1); conditions (random mating, no mutation, no selection, no gene flow, large population); deviations = evolutionary forces; genetic drift (Founder effect, Bottleneck effect); gene flow; adaptive radiation (Darwin's finches, Australian marsupials).

Out-of-Africa hypothesis; timeline: Dryopithecus (ape-like), Ramapithecus → Australopithecus (walked upright, small brain, Africa, 2 MYA) → Homo habilis (first tool use, 2 MYA) → Homo erectus (used fire, large brain, 1.5 MYA) → Homo heidelbergensis → Homo neanderthalensis (Ice Age, buried dead) → Homo sapiens (modern humans, 75,000-10,000 years ago); Cro-Magnon.

chapters.summary: Infectious diseases, immunity, cancer, AIDS, microbes in welfare — 4-5 questions annually in NEET.

topics.explanation: Diseases caused by pathogens — all must be known: Malaria (Plasmodium vivax/falciparum — female Anopheles mosquito vector, liver and RBC stages, drugs: chloroquine, quinine); Filariasis/Elephantiasis (Wuchereria bancrofti — Culex mosquito, lymph vessels blockage); Ascariasis (Ascaris lumbricoides — roundworm, faecal-oral, intestinal infestation); Typhoid (Salmonella typhi — contaminated food/water, Widal test, rose spots); Pneumonia (Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae — alveoli filled with fluid); Common cold (Rhinoviruses — nasal congestion, spreads by droplets/contact); Amoebiasis (Entamoeba histolytica — contaminated water, dysentery); Ringworm/Tinea (Microsporum, Trichophyton, Epidermophyton — fungi, dry scaly lesions); Dengue fever (dengue virus — Aedes aegypti mosquito, thrombocytopenia, high fever, two strains cause dengue haemorrhagic fever); Chikungunya (Chikungunya virus — Aedes mosquito, joint pain, fever).

Innate immunity: physical barriers (skin, mucus), cellular barriers (phagocytes — neutrophils, monocytes, macrophages, NK cells), physiological barriers (fever, acid in stomach), cytokine barriers (interferons); adaptive/acquired immunity: active (self-generated antibodies — lasting) vs passive (ready-made antibodies — temporary); primary and secondary immune response; humoral immunity: B-lymphocytes → plasma cells → antibodies (IgG, IgA, IgM, IgE, IgD); cell-mediated immunity: T-lymphocytes (helper T, cytotoxic T, suppressor T, memory T); MHC: HLA complex; vaccines: active immunisation (weakened/killed pathogens or their products); monoclonal antibodies.

Normal vs neoplastic cells; types: benign (encapsulated, localised) and malignant (invasive, metastasis); causes: physical carcinogens (UV, X-rays), chemical carcinogens (tobacco smoke, asbestos), biological carcinogens (oncogenic viruses — EBV, HPV); oncogenes (mutated proto-oncogenes — Ras, Myc, Src); tumour suppressor genes (p53 — 'guardian of genome', Rb); detection: biopsy, MRI, CT scan, immunological methods; treatment: surgery, radiation therapy, chemotherapy (anti-cancer drugs), immunotherapy.

HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) — retrovirus; genome: two RNA strands + reverse transcriptase; transmission: sexual contact, contaminated needles, blood transfusion, mother to child (vertical); pathogenesis: HIV infects CD4⁺ T-lymphocytes (helper T cells) and macrophages; uses reverse transcriptase to integrate into host DNA (provirus); gradual destruction of CD4⁺ cells → AIDS (CD4⁺ count < 200/μL); opportunistic infections; ELISA test for diagnosis; Western blot confirmatory; treatment: antiretroviral therapy (ART) — NRTIs, NNRTIs, protease inhibitors; no cure/vaccine.

Adolescence: physical, hormonal, psychological changes; drugs of abuse: opioids (heroin/smack — from Papaver somniferum, morphine acetylation, CNS depressant), cannabinoids (marijuana, hashish, ganja — from Cannabis sativa, THC), coca alkaloids (cocaine — from Erythroxylum coca, stimulant, dopamine rush); tobacco: nicotine (stimulant), CO and carcinogens (cancer), tar; alcohol abuse: liver cirrhosis, brain damage, social effects; consequences of drug abuse: addiction, withdrawal symptoms, crime, AIDS risk; prevention: education, counselling, peer pressure resistance.

Household food processing: Lactobacillus in curd formation; fermentation (bread — Saccharomyces cerevisiae), cheese (Propionibacterium — holes in Swiss cheese, Penicillium — Roquefort); industrial production: antibiotics (Penicillin — Penicillium notatum, discovered by Fleming; Streptomycin — Streptomyces), statins (Monascus purpureus), ethanol, citric acid (Aspergillus niger), enzymes, vitamins, bioactive molecules (cyclosporin A from Trichoderma polysporum); sewage treatment: primary (sedimentation), secondary (BOD reduction by aerobic microbes in aeration tank — activated sludge, anaerobic digestion in sludge digesters → biogas); biogas production: methanogens (Methanobacterium) from dung; biocontrol agents: Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) — Cry proteins as biopesticides, Trichoderma and Baculoviruses; biofertilizers: Rhizobium (symbiotic N-fixation in legumes), Azotobacter and Azospirillum (free-living N-fixation), blue-green algae (Anabaena, Nostoc — in paddy fields), mycorrhiza (Glomus — phosphate solubilisation).

chapters.summary: rDNA technology, PCR, transgenic organisms, gene therapy — 3-4 questions annually in NEET.

topics.topicName: Principles and Tools of Genetic Engineering
topics.explanation: Recombinant DNA technology (rDNA): basic principles (cutting, joining, introducing DNA into host); restriction endonucleases (restriction enzymes): Type II (EcoRI, HindIII, BamHI) — recognition sites (palindromes), sticky ends and blunt ends; gel electrophoresis: principle (DNA migration by size in agarose), Southern blotting, ethidium bromide staining, UV visualisation; cloning vectors: plasmid (pBR322 — ori, antibiotic resistance genes, restriction sites), bacteriophage lambda, cosmids, BAC, YAC; competent host (CaCl₂ treatment, electroporation); selectable markers (antibiotic resistance, insertional inactivation — blue-white screening).

Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR): principle (exponential amplification of target DNA); steps: denaturation (94°C), annealing (primers — 50-65°C), extension (Taq polymerase — 72°C); thermocycler; applications: forensics, disease diagnosis, evolutionary studies; bioreactor: scale-up of rDNA products; downstream processing: isolation, purification of product.

topics.topicName: Applications in Health — Insulin and Vaccines
topics.explanation: Genetically engineered insulin: Eli Lilly (1982) — humulin; human insulin gene cloned in E. coli; A and B chains produced separately → combined with disulphide bonds; comparison with animal insulin; vaccine production: hepatitis B vaccine (from yeast), subunit vaccines, edible vaccines (transgenic plants — banana, tomato); gene therapy: somatic gene therapy — ADA deficiency (SCID) — first human gene therapy (1990, Ashanthi DeSilva), vector-mediated gene delivery.

topics.topicName: Genetically Modified Organisms — Bt Crops and Transgenic Animals
topics.explanation: Transgenic plants: Bt cotton (Bacillus thuringiensis cry genes — Cry1Ac and Cry2Ab — encode Bt toxin δ-endotoxin — activated in alkaline insect gut → pore formation → insect death; pest: Bollworm); Bt corn (Cry1Ab — corn borer); FLAVR SAVR tomato (antisense RNA for polygalacturonase — delayed ripening); Golden Rice (beta-carotene synthesis — cry1, cry2, psy genes); transgenic animals: Rosie (cow with human α-lactalbumin), knock-out and knock-in mice (research models), transgenic sheep, pigs for xenotransplantation.

Biosafety issues: GMO risks (gene pollution, allergies, ecological balance disruption); GEAC (Genetic Engineering Appraisal Committee) in India — regulatory body; containment levels (BL1 to BL4); biopiracy: unauthorised use of biological resources and traditional knowledge by corporations (examples: Basmati rice — RiceTec USA patent contested, Turmeric patent challenged by India, Neem — W.R. Grace patent challenged); biodiversity and traditional knowledge protection; TRIPS (Trade-Related Intellectual Property Rights); CIPD; patents on life forms controversy.

chapters.summary: Population ecology, ecosystem, biodiversity conservation — 3-4 questions annually in NEET.

topics.explanation: Population attributes: birth rate (natality), death rate (mortality), age distribution (pre-reproductive, reproductive, post-reproductive; age pyramids — expanding, stable, declining), sex ratio; population growth: exponential growth (dN/dt = rN, J-shaped curve, unlimited resources), logistic growth (dN/dt = rN[(K-N)/K], S-shaped sigmoid curve, K = carrying capacity, intraspecific competition); population interactions — all types must be known: mutualism (+/+ — Rhizobium in legume roots, lichen, mycorrhiza, orchid bees), commensalism (+/0 — barnacles on whale, orchid on mango tree), amensalism (−/0 — Penicillium vs bacteria), competition (−/− — Gause's competitive exclusion principle, resource partitioning), predation (+/− — predator-prey cycles, co-evolution, camouflage, mimicry), parasitism (+/− — Cuscuta on host, Plasmodium, brood parasitism in birds).

Ecosystem structure: abiotic components (temperature, light, water, minerals) and biotic components (producers, consumers, decomposers); functional aspects; productivity: gross primary productivity (GPP — total photosynthesis), net primary productivity (NPP = GPP − respiration), secondary productivity; decomposition: detritus food chain; steps: fragmentation, leaching, catabolism, humification, mineralisation; factors affecting decomposition (temperature, humidity, O₂ availability, C:N ratio of detritus).

Trophic levels: producers (T1) → primary consumers (T2) → secondary consumers (T3) → tertiary consumers (T4); Lindeman's 10% law (only 10% energy transferred between trophic levels — rest lost as heat); food chain (grazing and detritus); food web; ecological pyramids: pyramid of number (can be inverted — tree ecosystem), pyramid of biomass (can be inverted — aquatic ecosystems), pyramid of energy (always upright, never inverted); production efficiency; standing crop.

Biogeochemical cycles — gaseous (atmospheric reservoir) and sedimentary (Earth's crust reservoir); carbon cycle: photosynthesis (fixation), respiration, combustion, decomposition, ocean as reservoir; nitrogen cycle: nitrogen fixation (biological — Rhizobium, Azotobacter; industrial — Haber process; atmospheric — lightning), ammonification, nitrification (Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter), denitrification (Pseudomonas) — complete cycle with organisms at each step; phosphorus cycle (sedimentary, no gaseous phase).

Concept of biodiversity: genetic diversity (different alleles within species), species diversity (variety of species), ecological diversity (variety of ecosystems); patterns: latitudinal gradients (species richness increases towards equator), species-area relationship (S = CA^z — MacArthur and Wilson); importance (direct values: food, drugs, aesthetic; indirect values: ecosystem services); threats: habitat loss (most important cause), overexploitation, alien species invasion, co-extinctions; global species: ~7 million total; India: 12 mega-biodiversity nations; conservation strategies: in-situ (on-site — national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, biosphere reserves, sacred groves, gene banks — most appropriate) and ex-situ (off-site — botanical gardens, zoos, cryopreservation, in vitro fertilisation); hotspots: Western Ghats + Sri Lanka, Himalayas (Indo-Burma), Indo-Burma, Sundaland — 34 global hotspots (2.5% of land but 50% of all species); IUCN categories: Extinct (EX), Extinct in Wild (EW), Critically Endangered (CR), Endangered (EN), Vulnerable (VU), Near Threatened (NT), Least Concern (LC); Red Data Book; biosphere reserves (UNESCO — 18 in India); protected areas (national parks — 100+ in India; wildlife sanctuaries — 500+ in India); Sacred Groves (traditional community protection of forest patches).
